title: Cryptographic Failures
summary: A session token carries your role, 'protected' five different broken ways — plaintext, base64, repeating-key XOR, a keyless md5, and a short-key HMAC — until an HMAC under a strong random key. Forge an admin token at each stage.
goal: Forge a session token the server accepts as role: admin and unlock the admin secret.
goalSecure: Try the earlier forgeries (plaintext edit, base64, XOR key recovery, md5 recompute, PIN brute force) — all should be rejected, while your genuine token still verifies.
rootCause: Token integrity is protected by encoding or weak/keyless/guessable-key primitives instead of a keyed MAC (or signature) under a strong secret, so an attacker can produce a valid token.
lessons: A token with no integrity protection can be rewritten to anything.
Encoding (base64) is not encryption — it's trivially reversible.
A homemade repeating-key XOR cipher falls to a known-plaintext attack.
A plain hash (md5) is not a MAC — without a key, anyone can recompute it.
HMAC is only as strong as its key — a short/guessable key is recovered offline.
Bind integrity with HMAC/signature under a strong random key, compared in constant time (AES-GCM for confidentiality).

stages.title: Plaintext token
stages.defense: None — the token is user|role in the clear.

stages.title: Repeating-key XOR
stages.defense: XORs the token with a fixed key, then base64.

stages.title: Weak HMAC key
stages.defense: HMAC-SHA256 with a short numeric key.

stages.title: HMAC with a strong random key
stages.defense: HMAC-SHA256 with a 256-bit random key; constant-time compare.
